title: Run a Stage 3 Science Fair
overview: Bring together everything from Stage 3 Science to run a science fair that teaches visitors the most important ideas from this stage through real investigations.

project.drivingQuestion: How can we use the science from Stage 3 to run an accurate, safe and exciting science fair for our school or community?
project.finalProduct: Create a science-fair plan with a live investigation, labelled displays, a safety checklist, a visitor survey with a data display, and a short explanation of your scientific decisions.

stages.title: 1. Foundations display
stages.prompt: Choose the two most important ideas from Units 1, 2. Build a display that teaches each idea with a model or diagram, an example and a check question.
stages.evidence: Two labelled displays with models, examples and check questions

stages.title: 2. Live investigation
stages.prompt: Pick one investigation from Units 3, 4 and run it live: state your hypothesis, follow the method, record results and explain your conclusion.
stages.evidence: A completed investigation with hypothesis, method, results and conclusion

stages.title: 3. Connections wall
stages.prompt: Create a connections wall that links ideas from Units 5, 6 to everyday life, with at least three labelled connections.
stages.evidence: A connections wall with three labelled links to daily life

stages.title: 4. Present and explain
stages.prompt: Present your science fair. Explain at least three scientific choices, check that your conclusions match your evidence and identify one improvement you would make.
stages.evidence: Spoken, written or recorded scientific explanation

evidenceChecklist: A safety checklist for every activity
A connections wall with three links
A survey with organised data display

rubric.secure: Observations, measurements, models and conclusions are accurate and checked.
Hypotheses, fair testing, recording and conclusions follow the scientific method.
Labels, diagrams, tables or charts make the science visible.
Decisions are explained using appropriate science words and evidence.

questions.question: Which of these is a living thing?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a camel. A camel does all seven life processes, so it is living. The others never lived.

questions.question: Which word list names the seven life processes?
options: big, small, hot, cold, wet, dry, hard
movement, respiration, sensitivity, growth, reproduction, excretion, nutrition
red, blue, green, tall, short, round, flat
happy, sad, tired, hungry, cold, warm, sleepy
questions.answer: movement, respiration, sensitivity, growth, reproduction, excretion, nutrition
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: movement, respiration, sensitivity, growth, reproduction, excretion, nutrition. These are the seven life processes, remembered as MRS GREN.

questions.question: What is a mixture?
options: Two or more materials together, each keeping its properties
questions.answer: Two or more materials together, each keeping its properties
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A mixture is two or more materials together, and each one keeps its own properties.

questions.question: When sugar dissolves in tea, the sugar:
options: breaks into tiny pieces spread through the tea
sinks and stays at the bottom
questions.answer: breaks into tiny pieces spread through the tea
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: The sugar breaks into pieces too small to see and spreads through the tea; it is not destroyed.

questions.question: Which of these is a natural light source?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the sun — it is not made by people.

questions.question: We can see objects because light ______.
options: bounces off them into our eyes
questions.answer: bounces off them into our eyes
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: light bounces off them into our eyes — we see reflected light.

questions.question: Which organ controls your thinking and memory?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Brain The brain is the control center for thinking and memory.

questions.question: What is the main job of your heart?
options: To pump blood around your body
questions.answer: To pump blood around your body
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Pump blood around your body The heart is a muscle that pushes blood everywhere.

questions.question: A force is best described as:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a force is a push or a pull.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a force?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the colour blue is not a force; it does not push or pull anything.

questions.question: What shape are the Earth, the Sun, and the Moon?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: spherical All three are round balls, not flat. Photos from space and everyday observation show this.

questions.question: Which of these is the biggest?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the Sun The Sun is by far the biggest; over a million Earths could fit inside it.
